summary:
The involvement of T-cells in periodontitis indicates delayed hypersensitivity plays a significant role due to their critical function in responding to microbial stimuli and driving inflammation leading to tissue destruction. This underscores how specific antigen-driven cellular interactions are essential for both initiating and perpetuating the condition at various stages from early gingival changes up until advanced periodontal breakdown.
To summarize Agent2’s reasoning into two sentences:

Periodontitis involves T-cell driven immune responses targeting bacteria, which contribute to tissue damage via osteoclastic activity; these findings support the occurrence of delayed hypersensitivity in the pathogenesis of periodontal diseases. Early signs include T-lymphocyte infiltration preceding other immune cell populations, highlighting their crucial initial role in triggering subsequent inflammatory processes.
